channel.title: Blog on ALTV!ST
channel.description: Recent content in Blog on ALTV!ST

item.title: A funny and useless OOB read in the iMelody parser (macOS)
item.description:
Recently I discovered, almost by accident, that:

  1. CoreAudio in macOS contains a parser for the iMelody format. This is a non-polyphonic ringtone format that Sony Ericsson developed for its mobile phones back in the first half of the 2000s. Don’t ask me why a parser for this format ever ended up in a macOS system library, and — more importantly — why it’s still there. I have no idea.

  2. This parser has a bug that leads to an OOB read. The bug has probably gone unnoticed for decades. Right now it’s completely useless from an exploitation standpoint, for several reasons I’ll get into below.

So get comfortable — this is going to be a short and fairly fun blog post (assuming your idea of fun is as peculiar as mine).

item.title: How to reassemble a C++ `switch` / `case` in x86_64
item.description: In this post I want to talk about how to correctly rewrite a disassembled C++ switch / case in assembly so that it can later be assembled back into machine code. I know the task sounds odd, but bear with me — I’ll explain why this is needed and what the actual problem is.

item.title: A bug in Apple Audio Toolbox that leads to heap OOB read
item.description:
Last month, while testing HOBO BN MCP, I found two very similar vulnerabilities in macOS. I reported both to Apple Product Security through the Apple Security Bounty program — one report per vulnerability. Apple confirmed one of them, and I’m now waiting for a CVE and the update that will patch it. Once the patch ships, I’ll publish a detailed write-up about that one.

The second report was rejected: “We’re unable to identify a security issue in your report.” Well — “rejected” may not be quite the right word. In all likelihood, this second vulnerability will also be fixed in a future update, just silently, with no CVE and no bounty.

As I mentioned, the two vulnerabilities are very similar, so Apple Product Security’s call on the second one came as an unpleasant surprise. But I’m not going to argue with Apple in this post. Instead, I’ll walk through the second vulnerability in detail and, at the end, share a few thoughts on why Apple Product Security might not have identified it as a security issue.

item.title: HOBO BN MCP v1.1.0 released
item.description: I was too stingy to pay $1499+ for a commercial Binary Ninja license and a pile of tokens that MCP waste every time I say “Hello!” to Claude. So I wrote my own MCP-like solution from scratch. Despite its name, HOBO BN MCP is not an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. Instead, HOBO BN MCP runs inside Binary Ninja as a plugin and uses a simple JSON-over-HTTP protocol.

item.title: Hello, world!
item.description: The traditional meaningless first post in almost any blog 🙂
